Thank you very much for your thoughtful response.

> should non-lexiographic tableau exist at all:
> I understand that part of the purpose of the library is being a teaching tool. From that perspective I think it really makes sense to offer both, as the lexiographic class sort of highlights that these are the few places you'd adapt lemke howson in order to handle degenerate matrices. At least for me just understanding the basic algorithm was already complex enough, and the theory around lexiographic sorting was a very cryptic layer on top of that again. So offering lex sort as an extension to the normal tableau makes the differences very explicit.

I agree. This will also help when I refactor the documentation on LH.

> what should the default/options be when calling `Game::lemke_howson_enumeration``:
I took the conservative approach of keeping the behavior exactly the same as today. I probably think you should decide what the most sensible defaults and options are. 

I appreciate the way you've put this PR together greatly :)

> I randomly stumbled into nash equilibriums and most of my cases were degenerate games. So I would maybe think that given the performance is quite similar but lex-sorting more versatile, I would lean towards at least default being lex sorting.

Let's do it! If you could make the change to make lex the default that'd be great.

> String algorithm, vs bool lexiographic option
I drew inspiration from https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/tutorial/optimize.html when going for string. Still I agree bool makes sense if there are just two different implementations. If you think that is the case, I'd be happy to make that change, though potentially I see there might be more algorithms like:
until I managed to solve the lex sorting bug I considered experimenting with the cycle-breaking approach of picking a different row to pivot each time we had the same basic variables and same label to drop
could there be other pivoting algorithms (potentially with or without lex sorting). What we do today maybe emphasizes numerical and integer stability, but could run into infs and nans on large matrices. Potentially there could be an algorithm that handles those types of matrices better.

Let's go for bool, I think we're on the same page here. I don't want to over optimise for future changes though so let's just go with what's nice now.

encoder: conv2d conv2d conv2d dense(256) # <- this 256 is non-intuitively set under the hood by RLlib because fcnet_hiddens - which has NOTHING to do with this setup - by default is [256, 256] and we take the -1th item :(

vf: dense(1)

To:

encoder: conv2d conv2d conv2d
pi: dense(256) dense([num_actions])
vf: dense(256) dense(1)
```

My intention here with accepting plain `AggregateFn` instead of `Aggregation` was [following the Pandas `.agg()` semantics](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.agg.html), where `Callable` or `List[Callable]` is applied to all columns. I.e., for the Pandas `.agg()` API, the structure of the arguments dictate the column(s) that the given aggregations will be applied to:
- `Union[str, AggregateFn, List[Union[str, AggregateFn]]]`: Aggregation(s) are applied to each column.
- `Dict[str, List[Union[str, AggregateFn]]]`: Aggregations are applied to the corresponding key column.

Since it's the structure of the arguments and not the arguments themselves that dictate the columns, accepting `AggregateFn`, which are not bound to a column, makes more sense to me **if our goal is to try to match the Pandas `.agg()` API**.

* add test runs of these benchmarks to CI
* add runs of these benchmarks to our main benchmarking configuration
* use the functionality from this pull request so that the code can work in two modes. https://github.com/modin-project/modin/pull/5015

At too small dimensions, where pandas can execute a function in a few tens of milliseconds, modin is unlikely to be able to overtake pandas. However, if you increase the dataset, you may encounter a lack of memory on the machine that runs these validation tests during the CI process. Because of this, the already added benchmarks have a built-in functionality that allows you to change the data size for benchmarks depending on these situations, but it is still manual, we need to manually select the size for these two situations.

Since the type hint is `Optional[Sequence[str]]`, I would expect this to use `__config__.get_object` like the following (and possibly cast the result to make mypy happy, if needed):

```suggestion
    def favorite_plants(self) -> Optional[Sequence[str]]:
        return __config__.get_object('favoritePlants')
```

Looking at AWS, for Node.js you can see it's doing the right thing:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/1b4fa4fecdc5b89c0141f07dcc9f65a4a1465d85/sdk/nodejs/config/vars.ts#L16

```typescript
export let allowedAccountIds: string[] | undefined = __config.getObject<string[]>("allowedAccountIds");
```

But for Python, it looks like we don't return the rich objects, we just always use `get` and return the string value (which for objects, will be a JSON string):

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/1b4fa4fecdc5b89c0141f07dcc9f65a4a1465d85/sdk/python/pulumi_aws/config/vars.py#L42

```python
allowed_account_ids = __config__.get('allowedAccountIds')
```

Unfortunately, I don't think we can change this without it being breaking for our existing providers. If we want to return actual objects, we'll probably want a Python setting in the schema so providers can opt-out (or opt-in depending on what we want the default behavior to be).

Or we just maintain the existing behavior and type all these as `Optional[str]`.

Dask does not know about the object store; this map can be thought of as "resources to pluck out of `resources` and give as top-level Ray task options under a new name", i.e. these are resources that we do not allow to be in the `ray.remote(resources={...})` argument and that _have_ to be given as top-level. Dask Distributed users are going to be used to giving their CPU and GPU resource requests as part of the `resources={...}` dict, either to `.compute()` or as an `.annotate()` annotation, so this mapping makes this transition a bit easier.

The other option is to let Ray throw an error when these resources are given in the `ray.remote(resources={...})` argument and make the user give `num_cpus`, `num_gpus`, etc., as top-level `.compute()` and `.annotate()` arguments. I'd like to give the user the option to keep all resource requests in the `resources={...}` dict since I think that's a nice, clean option, but happy to keep this more in line with our `ray.remote()` API.

Indentation and carriage returns are not possible in a on-the-fly python code execution like with `python -c`. And this needs to be a on-the-fly python code execution because it is passed to the `--manual-auth-hook` flag of certbot in a shell.

As I observed, the tests had never failed because of the absence of this check in certbot-ci. Also to consider that the ACME server is making several attempts on his side to retrieve the challenge, so it already covers the situation of the challenge not being immediately served by the HTTP server.

Alternative solution here would be to write the content of the script in a temporary location, then pass `python /temp/path/to/script.py` to `--manual-auth-hook`.

    environment variable ``INVENIO_INSTANCE_PATH`` is not be set.
    This directory is then automatically removed.
    """
    invenio_instance_path = os.environ.get('INVENIO_INSTANCE_PATH')
    invenio_static_folder = os.environ.get('INVENIO_STATIC_FOLDER')
    path = invenio_instance_path
    # static folder
    if not invenio_static_folder:
        if invenio_instance_path:
            os.environ['INVENIO_STATIC_FOLDER'] = os.path.join(
                invenio_instance_path, 'static')
        else:
            os.environ['INVENIO_STATIC_FOLDER'] = os.path.join(
                sys.prefix, 'var/instance/static')
    # instance path
    if not path:
        path = tempfile.mkdtemp()
        os.environ['INVENIO_INSTANCE_PATH'] = path
    yield path
    # clean static folder variable
    if not invenio_static_folder:
        os.environ.pop('INVENIO_STATIC_FOLDER', None)
    # clean instance path variable and remove temp dir
    if not invenio_instance_path:
        os.environ.pop('INVENIO_INSTANCE_PATH', None)
        shutil.rmtree(path)
```

According to https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#bytes.decode

```py
bytes.decode(encoding='utf-8', errors='strict')
```

Knack's current implementation assumes `o` is bytes representing a string. This may not be true and will even cause error if `o` is a binary stream that can't be UTF-8 decoded.

It will be safer to use [`hex`](https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#bytearray.hex)/[base16](https://docs.python.org/3/library/base64.html#base64.b16encode) or [base64](https://docs.python.org/3/library/base64.html#base64.b64encode) to represent a binary stream.

I prefer not to change the current (incorrect) behavior of Knack. Instead, you may consider serializing the bytearray by yourself utilizing a transform. This will give your more control of how the bytearray should be serialized:

IMO you should take `maxresults` into account here as well. Consider the use case where a library contains 5000 items and the user wants to retrieve the first 1000 in batches of 50 items. That would require 20 requests. Now you are making 100 requests and then throw away 4000 of the items.

Maybe something like this:
```python
maxresults = min(maxresults, self.totalSize)`
if maxresults <= len(results)
    break

args['X-Plex-Container-Start'] += args['X-Plex-Container-Size']
args['X-Plex-Container-Size'] = min(args['X-Plex-Container-Size'], maxresults - len(results))
```
This makes sure that you retrieve exactly the number of items requested. In case the user wants to retrieve all items you perform a few unnecessary calculations but those shouldn't be expensive compared to the (potentially unnecessary) HTTP requests.

And then you also don't need to slice the `results` list at the end.

This is the fix I (perhaps too briefly) mention above, with the improvements to ``assert_allclose``. Previously the incompatibility between the test image creation routine and the fitting routine meant that the test failed, essentially. This meant that, in order to pass the test, the tolerance had to be inflated significantly -- in this case with a tolerance of 100%, or do the input and output flux agree to a factor between 0.5 and 2. As the two functions (PDF Gaussian evaluated at pixel center vs integration over a pixel) weren't _that_ wrong this criterion was passed, but more or less resulted in a test that was wrong, but meaningless; a test with ``rtol=1`` might as well just not have been run in the first place.

I lowered this to 10% so that the test can now actually fail if incompatible changes are made to either routine which affect their results by a non-negligible but not massive amount. Unfortunately I now see that I did not remove the explaining comment for such a high tolerance, which should probably be removed to avoid confusion in the future.

This `self.unifinished_tasks += 1` ends up looking something like:
```
In [17]: class A:
    ...:     def __init__(self):
    ...:         self.unfinished_tasks = 0
    ...:     def put(self):
    ...:         self.unfinished_tasks += 1
    ...:

In [18]: dis.dis(A.put)
  5           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              2 DUP_TOP
              4 LOAD_ATTR                0 (unfinished_tasks)
              6 LOAD_CONST               1 (1)
              8 INPLACE_ADD
             10 ROT_TWO
             12 STORE_ATTR               0 (unfinished_tasks)
             14 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             16 RETURN_VALUE
```
Importantly here `INPLACE_ADD` at offset 8 ends up invoking the `__iadd__` protocol and thus expands to many bytecodes, but for integers this should hopefully be atomically implemented in C.  However it only gets stored again at offset 12.  So if GC runs a put here concurrently we could end up with an unfinished task when we think they're all finished.

Is that acceptable for us?

Tweaking the object and doing "re-init" is usually a good way of having bugs (see the `Tracer`…) because you have two different code paths to do your initialization (one for init, one for re-init).

This approach of doing a `copy()` has the upside of being clearer (yay functional programming. You just throw an object because you don't need it anymore (it's dead after a fork) and creates a new one by copying the existing one, which re-use the same init code path.

It also has the upside of splitting the profiler code and the fork handling in two different abstractions, making it easier to test only a part of the code at a time.

So yes I really stand by this design, unless missing something. :)

The main issue is I would like to address performing multiple `BLEAdapter.service_discovery` with multiple UUIDs in a program so I can discover single UUIDs on at a time. Currently, if you run `BLEAdapter.service_discovery` multiple times, you will most likely encounter this error message on the second `service_discovery`:
```
pc_ble_driver_py.exceptions.NordicSemiException: Failed to service_discovery. Error code: BLEGattStatusCode.invalid_handle
```
Then, if you retrieve this service list in your `BLEAdapter` object's `db_conns[conn_handle].service`, you will notice multiple instances of the same `uuid` with the same `start_handle` and `end_handle`. What my pull request was addressing was to mitigate these duplicates by checking if the UUIDs already existed within the `db_conns[conn_handle]` services, it's characteristics, and it's descriptors. This would allow multiple `BLEAdapter.service_discovery` methods to be run at any point, even with the same UUIDs so that multiple service handles do not exist and no errors will occur.

What happens if we have a repeatable directive on `FIELD_DEFINITION` and it is applied twice to the same field? Is the lexicographic sorting function going to order those two occurrences of the directive in a specific way?

Presumably, the following:
```graphql
type Foo {
    my_field: Int @custom_directive(b: "456", a: 1) @custom_directive(b: "123", a: 23)
}
```
after lexicographic sorting should turn into
```graphql
type Foo {
    my_field: Int @custom_directive(a: 23, b: "123") @custom_directive(a: 1, b: "456")
}
```
and it would be good to verify that schemas like that have the same fingerprints with a test.

Separately, it might be a good idea to check if the GraphQL-core library has tests like this for the lexicographic ordering function, and contribute them in a PR if not.

@telamonian You are right. When it comes to theming as is wanted in JupyterLab, you don't have much of a choice apart from loading different theme assets via CSS (as is currently down now in this PR for dark and light modes).

However, one needs to be careful in ensuring that the use of SVG icons does not cause the DOM tree to explode. For example, were we to use SVG injection here (for branch icons) and we had `1000` branches, assuming, for a moment, no virtual scrolling/windowing, then we'd inject 1000 SVG icons into the DOM. Upon doing so, performance, in particular DOM tree traversal, will be adversely affected based on the principle that, yes, more node => more overhead => more bad.

That this principle affects JupyterLab more generally is supported by the fact that JupyterLab exhibits poor performance for large notebooks (e.g., due to the number of DOM nodes associated with each input and output cell).

In this case, particularly when an image is going to be repeated many times within a document, leveraging CSS is better (perf-wise), despite being more limited/restrictive for theming.

For what you are doing with JLab where you can expect, e.g., toolbar icons to render once, or at most a handful times, in the application, using SVG icons is fine. Issues arise, however, when SVG nodes proliferate within an HTML document--a point to which the web data visualization community can readily attest.

The code for `grid_search` is very simple:

```
def grid_search(values: Iterable) -> Dict[str, List]:
    """Convenience method for specifying grid search over a value.

    Arguments:
        values: An iterable whose parameters will be gridded.
    """
    return {"grid_search": values}
```

That said, it is an internal representation and users should always use `tune.grid_search`. We may change this representation at some point.

The `List` can be used for any kind of nested value, so it's recursive `List[SampleRange]` (which is invalid to define). That said it will yield a list, so if none of our properties are lists, we can remove it here.

For the question at hand, users should always just use `tune.grid_search`, but we need to allow `dict` here so IDEs play nicely with it.

I was doing some tests of this locally myself, and discovered an interesting bug. With the `--json` flag turned on, I noticed that `response_details` was still always empty, even when there should have been something in there (e.g. 404 still has content to display).

What I discovered is that the `requests` library's `Response` objects actually evaluates as "falsy" when the status code is an error. Checkout the two examples below:

```python
import requests

resp = requests.get('https://api.github.com/users/travishathaway')

print(resp)   # <Response [200]>
print(bool(resp))   # True

resp = requests.get('https://api.github.com/users/asfaaasdfasdfasdf')

print(resp)   # <Response [404]>
print(bool(resp))  # False
```

Any who, this is all a really long-winded way of saying that you should use a strict `None` check in that if statement to avoid this situation 😅 

```python
response_details = (stringify(response, content_max_len=1024) or "") if response is not None else ""
```

Did you change the shape calculation rule described right below this function's header? This shape calculator requires that your classifier to have [N, C] input shape, so you need to accept [N, C] and reshape it into [C] where N is always 1 in your graph. Also, there are always two outputs, [one for label](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.naive_bayes.BernoulliNB.html#sklearn.naive_bayes.BernoulliNB.predict) and the other [one for probability](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.naive_bayes.BernoulliNB.html#sklearn.naive_bayes.BernoulliNB.predict_proba). Consequently, I don't think the shape calculation rules should be changed here.
Note that an important concept is that shape calculator determines the input and output shape of your graph, so the associated converter should make sure the produced sub-graph meets the shapes produced here.

Line 85 is intended to handle this case, by rejecting any subscriptions after the first.

(This aligns with Subscriber Rule 5 here: https://github.com/reactive-streams/reactive-streams-swift.)

The intent is to never accept any subscription after the first. It's generally up to the user not to attach one Subscriber to multiple Publishers, but the API design helps here by encouraging the use of operations (like `Map`) that instantiate a Subscriber and then attach to it all at once. In short, by having factory functions on Publisher that both generate the next pipe in the pipeline and connect it immediately, yielding a new Publisher.

I don't think we're going to be extending this class, so name mangling as a tool to avoid collisions doesn't really help us here.

But I do mean this method to be at class scope. I don't *want* this method to be re-usable elsewhere unless it is officially promoted to part of the API (and given a test, etc.). At that point we can rename it with find/replace because... it won't be mentioned anywhere else in the codebase. I could use some kind of custom decorator that audits the call stack to make sure nobody is calling it who shouldn't be, but the two underscores is standard, easier, and less obnoxious if someone *does* have to hack around it for a good reason.

I think when I was refactoring the leader I got mad about how the particular behavior of single-underscore-prefixed stuff had grown to matter in other parts of the codebase. I wanted to be able to change the semantics of or drop a thing that started with an underscore, but it turned out it was being used elsewhere or under test in the unit tests. The `AbstractJobStore` even has e.g. `_read_from_url()` as part of its defined API, which concrete job store classes are required to implement.

Maybe if I was using a proper IDE that knew about all the project files at once, I wouldn't trip over these usages because the IDE would be surfacing them for me?

Anyway, now when I add something that I don't want to grow secret connections to other parts of the codebase over time, I put two underscores on it.

Hi -- as I said before, we don't restrict the type of Music21Object that can call .show() in a particular format (except that the testing for Opus objects leaves something to be desired -- but that's a bug).  For instance:

```
In [1]: n = note.Note()                                                         

In [2]: n.show('text')                                                          
<music21.note.Note C>

In [3]: n.show('lily')                                                          
GNU LilyPond 2.20.0
...
Success: compilation successfully completed
```

The solution is to first check, "if not obj.isStream" and put it into a Measure.  Then check if it is a generic Stream, if not, put all its elements in a Measure.  Then check if it's a Measure and put it in a Part.  Then check if it's a Part, and then put it in a Score.  Then you're ready to show a standard Score.   Again, look at how musicxml writing works -- best always to start with a check for the largest deficiencies (like it's a Note object) and then move up from there.  

But note that RomanText allows more than one score to be defined in an `.rntxt` file and are parsed as an Opus (see `romanText.translate.romanTextToStreamOpus`) so the output format should be Opus-aware also.

Note to reviewer: This is a second utility function building on top of `get_initial_payment_for_final_target_amount`. 

While `get_initial_payment_for_final_target_amount` takes the target amount you want to finally reach the target as input, this function here `get_amount_for_sending_before_fees` takes the amount we want to leave the initiator. Essentially how much we need the initiator's balance to be reduced.

This is useful for example in cases where we want to see what should the "send_value" be to spend the entire deposit.

The function returns the `amount_to_send`, which is what should go as the send transfer amount, a list of all the mediators fees as returned by `get_initial_payment_for_final_target_amount` and the total `amount_with_fees`.

Important note:

`amount_with_fees` can also be bigger than `amount_to_send + sum(mediators_cut)`. That is since we also have the "internal routing" fee estimation which adds a percentage over the entire transfer amount and that can end up being bigger than what the mediators would withhold.

Don't set `allow_records=True` in `_numpy.py:215` (the `array_ufunc` method). It was for that exact case that `allow_records` was introduced, to set it to `False`.

You are correctly setting `allow_records=True` in `structure.py:1021`.

The reason it's a parameter is so that functions like `concatenate` can set it to True while `array_ufunc` can set it to False. NumPy's ufuncs are not allowed to descend through RecordArrays unless a customization is defined, so that's why it was set to False in `array_ufunc`, and that's what the failing test was supposed to catch (and it did catch it!). But I think it makes sense for `concatenate` to descend through RecordArrays—the same motivation isn't there. (You can't overload `concatenate` the way you can overload ufuncs.)

> So I think we should probably be explicit and assign new returned partitions to _partition field of the core Dataframe. Does this make sense?

Don't know. I'm a bit tensed about this approach as well as about returning "finalized" partitions from `PartitionManager.finalize`. IMHO, assigning new `._partitions` is redundant and would confuse developers as the line literally means `self._partitions = self._partitions` but creates an impression of newly created "finalized" array of partitions. I don't want to be a blocker here as the discussion we had in the PR is already quite big, but I still not really like the proposed approach because of its counterintuitivity.

So would like to hear other opinions from @modin-project/modin-core about this thread and I will just agree with the majority.

The questions are:
1. [`PartitionManager.finalize`](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/270fd697a82d051f0ab2c54b70b74296699bdcf2/modin/core/dataframe/pandas/partitioning/partition_manager.py#L1275-L1286) modifies the passed partition's state by calling the deferred calls on them (no new partitions produced during this), is this okay to return the passed partitions in the result for the sake of interface aligning with other manager's method? My concern is that it creates a false impression of the function's user that the returned array is a new one.
    ```python
    @classmethod
    def finalize(cls, partitions):
        [partition.drain_call_queue() for partition in partitions.flaten()]
        return partitions # return exactly the same object that was passed into the func
    ```
    (we can fix the actual issue (#4273) without that by just inspecting the wrapped method's argument for partitions to wait)
2. If we decide to return partitions from `PartitionManager.finalize`, should we then update `._partitions` with the new ones (actually exactly the same objects) that were returned from `PartitionManager.finalize`? My concern is that it could be really misleading, as the statement would literaly mean `self._partitions = self._partitions`:
    ```python
    def finalize(self):
        self._partitions = self._partition_mgr_class.finalize(self._partitions)
    ```

> 2.Notifier is using Tasklets which are posted rather than executed immediately. The virtual time simulator implemented in #2508 schedules all node events and transmissions to preserve the execution order in time. However, using Tasklets to send events breaks the strict ordering of events, thus could have impact on the correctness of simulation.

Can you please help explain why we need immediate eventing for OTNS to work (e.g., need to know exactly when RLOC16 or role value changes).
- The virtual time simulator in OT is emulating the platform layer and events are related to radio/timer, etc. 
- OpenThread core itself can be considered a big state machine which requires calls from platform layer and its public APIs to proceed, take any action and  move from one state to another.  
- So a simulator pretty much knows and is in control of when OT state changes (since it would be calling the platform/public APIs that cause OT to change). 
- A higher level simulator  can then use existing public APIs to query info/state from OT (e.g., check the current role using `otThreadGetDeviceRole()`). This can be done after every call that would change OT. 
- With such a model we should not need any immediate evening from OT. Can we  consider adopting such a model?

My reservation is for this exact purpose. For now the `OtnsStatus` may be placed where the callbacks are, but if we allow and use `OtnsStatus` directly in the core modules, it may be used in places where there is no callback and adds dependency in the code. 

I would highly suggest we funnel OTNS events through existing public APIs and/or adding new APIs/mechanism.

    assert str(awkward1.typeof(listoffsetarray.rpad(5, 1))) == "var * ?float64"           # passes

Actually, it would be reasonable to start `ListArray::rpad_and_clip` with `toListOffsetArray64()` and let `ListOffsetArray::rpad_and_clip` do the work. This class has to provide serialization mechanism so that scripted module can be saved.
Ideally, the underlying `SentencePieceProcessor` class should provide such mechanism, but it does not provide interface to serialize the model as protobuf.
Note 1. The lifecycle of the official `SentencePieceProcessor` is 1. train and save to a file then 2. load from the saved file. It does not have a functionality to save the model.
Note 2. If you look at [the source code of `SentencePieceProcessor`](https://github.com/google/sentencepiece/blob/master/src/sentencepiece_processor.h#L462), it almost look like provide serialization interface, but `ModelProto` and all the functionality related to `protobuf` does not exist in the library file.

To workaround this, we need to keep the content of the saved model file (model data serialized protobuf) when loading a model from a file.
So, it is simpler to pass the model content as string to constructor and to instantiate the underlying `SentencePieceProcessor` there. Because, you still need to pass the model content separately for the later serialization.

I guess we weren't using this fixture, but the use of the `importlib_resources` library to resolve filepaths was very intentional. I am not sure if it is relevant to the use case here, though. Did you try using the resources interface?

In general, the resources interface is the "correct" way these days in Python to reference paths to static files in a python package, instead of trying to construct them from os.path or Pathlib. The resources interface works for _all_ package types, which the others do not (think zip packages and in-memory runtimes, such as when using pyoxidizer).

In general, I think we should be careful about trying to "fix" Git rather than just augmenting it for the multi-repo stuff.

A lot of thought has gone into how it works, and we probably can't do better if we try. Its behavior makes sense ("hey, I couldn't update this thing, but I'll give you what I could do and let you fix the rest"). It's what people used to Git expect. We shouldn't surprise them too much, because losing them would be worse than losing the people who can't be bothered to learn a tiny bit of Git.

That said, we should write awesome docs, because people way too often blame the users when it's their own fault for not documenting things well. Assume your users are awesome and do the technically right thing, and write good docs. :)

For https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/west/issues/59, I agree that it's the right thing. If you're working on something on a separate branch, you're often expecting that it won't rebase cleanly, so west shouldn't try to rebase it for you in that case.

> Will this work even if the text file is edited when a jupyter server isn't running?

Indeed! You're free to close the server. There's no magic here: when the notebook is opened or refreshed, Jupytext reads the two files and merges inputs+outputs. The ipynb file is not modified when the notebook is read, but only the next time it is saved.

If you keep the notebook open, the extra bonus is that variables are preserved when you refresh the notebook. I'll add a question about that.

> e.g., if I have a notebook and paired markdown file, I synchronize them, and push them both to GitHub. Then somebody else updates just the markdown file and pushes the changes to GitHub. I pull in the changes, and turn on JupyterLab...what happens?

What happens is what you expect: you get the latest input cells from the markdown file, matched with outputs from the ipynb file. It will work 100% if you don't push the ipynb file to GitHub, and only 99% (*) if you do push the ipynb file.

Let me explain: Jupytext in Jupyter is very strict about the assumption that Jupyter always writes the ipynb file *before* the md file. If git happens to write the ipynb more than [one second](https://github.com/mwouts/jupytext/blob/872c6585738d4df252c41b21feb40a19b95a1c8b/jupytext/contentsmanager.py#L131-L136) after the md file, Jupytext will complain and refuse to open the notebook.

`DdogProfExporter` is an opaque wrapper class over libdatadog's `ddog_prof_Exporter`.   Every instance of `ddog_prof_Exporter` is under the management of libdatadog, so they are returned as pointers.  I wanted to consolidate the C-isms of the class into RAII, so

1. `DdogProfExporter` calls the appropriate libdatadog functions to create and destroy the `ddog_prof_Exporter` instance.  It also includes a few string-invariants which are only sent to libdatadog during instantiation of `ddog_prof_Exporter`.
2. the `Uploader` class takes a unique_ptr to `DdogProfExporter`.  This is mostly done to defer initialization of the field, so it occurs once inside of the constructor.

The lifetime and scope of `Uploader` and `DdogProfExporter` are comparable, so splitting the wrapper into its own class is a question of aesthetics, as far as I can tell.

found out that it's very useful to know amount of rows that every partition will read, instead of lines (this is critical for example for that issue #1067). 

Also found out that it's really challenging to write fast regexp that would count amount of `\n` that not in quotes, so for now I'm gonna use that approach with line counting in a for loop.

Here are time measurements of three approaches of rows counting, one with a for loop and other two with different regexpes:

<details><summary>reproducer</summary>


```python
import numpy as np
import string
import csv
import os
import re
from timeit import default_timer as timer

RAND_LOW = -100
RAND_HIGH = 100
N = 500
M = 128
TEST_FILENAME = os.path.abspath(f"int_dataset-{N},{M},{RAND_LOW},{RAND_HIGH}.csv")


def generate_data_file(filename, row_n, col_n):
    letters = list(string.ascii_letters)
    columns = [
        "".join(np.random.choice(letters) for _ in np.arange(10))
        for _ in np.arange(col_n)
    ]

    with open(filename, "w") as file:
        writer = csv.writer(file, delimiter=",")
        writer.writerow(columns)
        for _ in np.arange(row_n):
            writer.writerow(np.random.randint(RAND_LOW, RAND_HIGH, col_n))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if not os.path.exists(TEST_FILENAME):
        generate_data_file(TEST_FILENAME, N, M)
    path = TEST_FILENAME

    # regexp_0: if match has nonempty group №4, then this is
    # a new row. This method has disadvantage that we must iterate
    # over all of the matches and count only these which contains not
    # empty group №4
    regexp_0_groups = b"((\"[^\"]*\")|(.*))(.*$)"

    # regexp_1: maths all end of lines that's outside of quotes.
    # Disadvantage: cause of regex complexity it works really slow
    regexp_1_findall = b"$(?=(?:[^\"]*\"[^\"]*\")*[^\"]*\Z)"

    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        t1 = timer()
        outside_quotes = True
        nrows = 0
        for line in f:
            if line.count(b"\"") % 2:
                outside_quotes = not outside_quotes
            if outside_quotes:
                nrows += 1
        print("rows counting in a loop:", timer() - t1)

    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        t1 = timer()
        content = f.read()
        nrows = 0
        it = re.finditer(regexp_0_groups, content, re.MULTILINE)
        for x in it:
            g1, g2 = x.group(4), x.group(1)
            if (g1 is not None and g1 != b"") or (g2 is not None and g2 != b""):
                nrows += 1
        print("groups regexp: ", timer() - t1)

    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        t1 = timer()
        content = f.read()
        nrows = len(re.findall(regexp_1_findall, content, re.MULTILINE))
        print("findall", timer() - t1)

```


</details>

--

Also worth noting that I don't anticipate `common.py` growing any larger. I'm actively working to remove the complexity I added to the [setup repo's version](https://github.com/pantsbuild/setup/blob/gh-pages/build-support/common.py) by converting `ci.py` into a proper unit test file. I don't anticipate `common.py` ever having more functionality than what `common.sh` has. For example, if scripts need functionality from `dirutil.py`, those individual scripts will just directly depend on Pants.

Initialization order
The order of member initializers in the list is irrelevant: the actual order of initialization is as follows:

1) If the constructor is for the most-derived class, virtual bases are initialized in the order in which they appear in depth-first left-to-right traversal of the base class declarations (left-to-right refers to the appearance in base-specifier lists)
2) Then, direct bases are initialized in left-to-right order as they appear in this class's base-specifier list
3) Then, non-static data member are initialized in order of declaration in the class definition.
4) Finally, the body of the constructor is executed

The most up to date RFC is https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2046, but I agree it specifies that there must be at least one body part.
I would argue however that this RFC does not allow representing a multipart body with *no* body part, so we can't follow the RFC in this case: adding an open-close boundary would be parsed as "single empty body part" by any server - which is of course the expected behaviour - so imho it is not an option here.

I definitely agree with @asvetlov that aiohttp should mimic existing librairies here:
 - urllib3 (and requests) serialise empty body with a lone closing boundary: https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/master/src/urllib3/filepost.py#L63
 - the `email.message` module in the stdlib does indeed follow the RFC: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/email/generator.py#L291-L304 . But the consequence of this is that a "serialize-parse" loop with the stdlib implementation is not a no-op…

Since `resolved` is a list of the tokens in the document with white space, line 96 replaces the first coreference token with the mention it refers to, while lines 97-98 goes through and masks out all subsequent tokens with `""`, so that they are ultimately eliminated in the `"".join` that follows. See my comment regarding the previous function and pymatgen vs atomate

Ideally, atomate routines should be short and just glue together existing functionality in pymatgen into a set of Fireworks and Workflow objects. i.e. call a bunch of pymatgen functions and wrap that into a Firework/Workflow object.

When a lot of algorithm work is put in atomate, two problems occur:
* As atomate is less used than pymatgen, fewer people and developers review, vet, and improve the algorithms
* Generally, tying together workflows is confusing enough code for people to follow as workflows are somehow inherently confusing. When you mix in algorithm work together with the workflow stuff (even if the algorithms are in a separate private function), wrapping your head around workflows becomes even harder. So to keep atomate workflows easy to maintain, it's best if they are as short as possible and use pymatgen for most of the heavy lifting

I stumbled upon this PR because I saw `--use-deprecated=legacy-resolver` in the Dockerfile above. This will allow for conflicting additions/changes in those downstream files to go through unnoticed in CI, and I would recommend against it.

Regarding the diverging @amogkam, and the addition of `-c/--constraint` across the place in this PR: I would also recommend against it. In general, python libraries (not applications) should have compatibility ranges in their `install_requires` and `extras_require`, and only use exact pins if absolutely necessary due to a specific reason best documented inline.

That said, a lockfile containing a known working set of exhaustive dependencies is nice to have checked into git, and probably even necessary to keep CI reproducible. At work, we generate them with [pipgrip](https://github.com/ddelange/pipgrip).

We specify compatibility ranges for all our deps whenever possible:
- `requests~=2.11` which is equivalent to `requests>=2.11,<3`
- `requests~=2.11.1` which is equivalent to `requests>=2.11.1,<2.12`
to allow interoperability between our libs.

For local development and CI of a lib, we also have a file checked in with the latest known working set. If any txt file in the `./requirements` folder changes in a PR, running a refresh of the lockfile is required by CI. This has numerous advantages, and in practice avoids so much dephell and wasted dev hours, because finding the downstream culprit becomes more or less trivial.

For the devs we have `make lockfile` and `make install`:
```Makefile
.PHONY: install
## Install this repo, plus dev requirements, in editable mode
install:
	pip install -r requirements/ci.txt -r requirements/docs.txt -e .[all] --constraint=requirements/pipgrip.lock

.PHONY: lockfile
## Create a lockfile for all python requirements.
lockfile:
	pipgrip -vv --sort --lock --tree .[all]
	mv pipgrip.lock requirements/pipgrip.lock
```

All our `[extras]` have their own file in that folder, and we have ci.txt and docs.txt separate (not specified in setup.py), because they are not needed by an end user doing `pip install .[all]`.

I think a similar setup would save ray maintainers a lot of future headache. 

Hope that helps shed some light on dephell for big libraries with many extras :)

`str.join()` never has any effect because all the params have exactly one item in the list. I'd like to make two points here:
1) If two proxies can be present, we should actually test this case: there's no point in implying that this is the case but not checking it.
2) In tests, we must do as few runtime-dependent changes / conditional coding as possible. The cases should be statically defined because adding clever code to tests opens up more possibilities to add bugs in those tests. They should be as simple as possible. So instead of having a list of one item that is concatenated into the same one item, it's better to (1) remove `str.join()`, (2) turn lists into strings and (3) just have a string with two proxies and a comma in between in the specific param.

I think that is more specifically talking about integer data that is in the file as integers and needs to be scaled (ex. `data * scale_factor + add_offset`) to floats, but also has a fill value. In that case you'd need to do the `.where` call to mask out the floats with some other value. In Satpy that is NaN for floating point data and any `_FillValue` for integer data. In older versions of numpy (I think newer versions have fixed this) if you do `.where` with `np.nan` it would cast 32-bit floats to 64-bit floats. Now it knows that you really mean 32-bit float -> 32-bit float NaN. For integers, they would always be cast to 64-bit floats so the note in the documentation is trying to say "there is no reason for that so force 32-bit float conversion".

The note should probably be updated to refer to integer fields that are meant to stay integers like cloud type products or in this case 8-bit unsigned integer RGB images. If an RGB is being read in by Satpy and is provided as floating point data (instead of as an integer) then it should probably be normalized to a 0-1 range as that is the assumption by the trollimage package when saving images.

So, If `self.digits` is `None`, we need to pass a default value to classification_report:

```python
if self.digits:
    print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred, digits=self.digits))
else:
   print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred, digits=4))

or, we can set default value in `__init__` method:

```python
self.digits = digits or 4

The purpose of using md5 in all of these places is only for quick identification, and to have reasonable assurance that we have the "right" artifact, in the context of distinguishing if the package `flask-1.0.2-py_0.tar.bz2` in the package cache came from `free`, `main`, `conda-forge`, or some other channel.  We know pretty close to what it should be by the file name, but because we don't include channel information with the bare tarballs in the package cache, we don't know what channel they come from.  It's also a quick check to ensure that a package hasn't been updated in-place in the remote channel (and therefore needs to be downloaded again).

Matching the md5 to the expected md5 from repodata gives us the assurance that an artifact that has the right name in a package cache is actually the artifact that we want.  To be clear: **Using md5 here is not a security measure.**  Just as we use md5 as a checksum to ensure downloads have not been _inadvertently_ corrupted, we use md5 here for a fast check to make sure the artifact is as we expect (or not).

Neither md5 nor sha256 in this context is sufficient as a security measure to guard against malicious attacks.  The purpose of using md5 here is ONLY as a quick checksum.  And in these quick checksum cases we should prefer md5 to sha256 since the former is indeed quite a bit faster.

When we add cryptographic signatures, we will be making specific and more targeted use of sha256 + size, in combination.

The danger of running multiple processes with the same stdout/stderr is that you get their output unhelpfully intermingled. You know, `process oprocess two outputne output` type of thing. :-) But most things use line buffering for terminal output, and even when they don't, a human can usually reconstruct what happened. So, I can see how letting multiple processes write to the same stdout/stderr could be more useful-in-practice that it is unsafe-in-theory.

Meanwhile, running multiple processes with the same _stdin_ is almost always a terrible idea; if there's contention on reading from it, you get a pretty bad user experience, like only half your keystrokes seeming to work.

I agree with your point about it being easy to notice you failed to capture the output you wanted to capture (because those bytes you're about to inspect will be `None` instead) and hard to notice you accidentally captured the output you didn't want to capture. It's actually really obvious with that lens that my previous default was misguided, so thanks for laying it out like that!

How about:
* The defaults are `stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL, stdout=None, stderr=None`. These can be overridden by the user passing `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` arguments.
* `capture_stdout=True` means `stdout=subprocess.PIPE` and errors if a different `stdout` was specified by the user. Likewise for `capture_stderr=True` and `stderr=subprocess.PIPE`.
* For `subprocess.run()` compatibility, `capture_output=True` is equivalent to `capture_stdout=True, capture_stderr=True`.
* `input=<some bytes>` means `stdin=subprocess.PIPE` and tells the parent Trio process to write those bytes to the pipe, then close it. `passthrough_stdin=True` means `stdin=None`. It's an error to specify more than one of `input`, `passthrough_stdin`, and `stdin`.
* `input=None` is treated equivalently to "you didn't specify any input" and still means `stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL`. This is a discrepancy from `subprocess.run()`, but at least it fails fast. If we don't like that, we could have `input=None` be an error, and make the default be some private marker object instead of `None` in order to be able to distinguish.

Docker's pretty inconsistent in what it calls things.  Since dockerode is a wrapper around the Docker API, I start with the API docs.  So corresponding to the link in your comment, the createContainer docs are at https://docs.docker.com/engine/api/v1.37/#operation/ContainerCreate.  That has both Volumes and HostConfig.Binds.  I think Volumes just mounts empty temporary directories (that will be destroyed when the container exits) while Binds connects host directories to container directories:

> A list of volume bindings for this container. Each volume binding is a string in one of these forms:
> 
>     host-src:container-dest to bind-mount a host path into the container. Both host-src, and container-dest must be an absolute path.
>     host-src:container-dest:ro to make the bind mount read-only inside the container. Both host-src, and container-dest must be an absolute path.
>     volume-name:container-dest to bind-mount a volume managed by a volume driver into the container. container-dest must be an absolute path.
>     volume-name:container-dest:ro to mount the volume read-only inside the container. container-dest must be an absolute path.

We've now updated this to have an `args` param and a `opts` param, but the problem still lives. `args` will have some overlap with `opts` and I think defaulting to the most specific one passed into the args is fine as long as we're consistent. To consider these methods and what they do:

1. you can always create a bare-bones local workspace and configure it however you want. Use `LocalWorkspace.create(opts)` for this. This is the minority use-case.
2. Create a stack that will use a local program `LocalWorkspace.createStack(localArgs, opts)`. `localArgs` is essentially the required subset of opts necessary to stand up a stack with a local program.
3. Create a stack that will use an inline program `LocalWorkspace.createStack(inlineArgs, opts). Once again, `inlineArgs` is roughly the subset of `opts` required to get a functional stack ready to do updates.

Done. There was one change that I wanted to mention:

> and at what max_k_points they start to give same results.

The information that was in the docstring was incorrect. It said "This implementation approaches Dynasor implementation for number of max_k_points around 50000, but is also faster." I don't know where that came from. [By default, Dynasor uses 20,000 points.](https://gitlab.com/materials-modeling/dynasor/-/blob/1.1/dynasor#L146-147) The scaling should be exactly the same since we're using the same probability function for each k vector to ensure that reciprocal space is sampled isotropically. However, this also depends on the value of `k_max`, because for smaller values of `k_max` we can sample the entire grid in reciprocal space up to the target magnitude of k. That's met by [this condition for skipping the pruning step](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/aba73322c379a232aed8ea9b69fb4ebb4b4d376d/cpp/diffraction/StaticStructureFactorDirect.cc#L186-L190).

The parameter names here differ from the base method they're overriding, so if you wanted to specify a parameter name at the call site, you needed to use a different parameter name based on the type of the variable through which you were making a call.  On top of that, if you passed in an invalid argument, you would frequently get an exception giving you a different name than what was actually in the metadata for the derived type, because the exception would be coming from the base method.  And having different names meant everyone ended up needing to write and rewrite their own validation logic, whereas when everyone appropriately used the same name, it could all be put into a helper method that everyone then uses and which is now exposed publicly (and we could write a stream validation test suite that asserted those names for all streams).

Combining the responses from multiple requests with different ranges must be done only if there is a strong validator, such as a non-weak etag (cf. section 3.3 of RFC 7234 ("Combining Partial Content") and section 4.3. of RFC 7233 ("Combining Ranges")).
So to be on the safe side, if there is no (non-weak) etag, there should be no retry.

(To see why, imagine a server response that dynamically generates some json data that does not guarantee the same order of keys in the json objects on the next request: this is a valid server behavior, but the client here would produce corrupt data).

I think we should not change the `SPINEL_PROTOCOL_VERSION_THREAD_MINOR`.

- The policy for spinel is to ensure backward compatibility when adding new fields and properties (i.e. newer NCP image should stay compatible with older host side drive).
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/f8f75d6d05064fd63e81a3ad4518e5b3728303f8/src/lib/spinel/spinel.h#L295-L306

- We don't have a explicit policy for updating the spinel version number, but the idea for it is that it would change when there is bigger change that does not gurantee backward comatibility.
- **Changing the version can add new restrictions in product integration and firmware update (between host firmware and NCP/RCP firmware ).**


Here is the challenge with Posix/RCP model if we change the `VERSION_THREAD_MINOR`.
- The host code (radio spinel) does check the version of RCP to match its own version and otherwise it would exit with an error.
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/f8f75d6d05064fd63e81a3ad4518e5b3728303f8/src/lib/spinel/radio_spinel_impl.hpp#L232-L252
- So with this existing code, changing the VERSION_MINOR would mean, whenever POSIX code is updated on host side, we need to also update the RCP firmware along with it.

We have similar check in `wpantund` which checks the version (and also in other internal drivers we use on products (RTOS driver)) which mean we would need to update `wpantund` on host with any change on NCP firmware (which is not desirable).

which are specified. Implementation details generally extend to
“everything not explicitly mandated by the spec”, but sometimes it can
be helpful to call these out explicitly. This is well established
practice (e.g., `java.util.Map` re: null entries; `java.util.Comparable`
re: compatibility with `equals`; Python <3.7 re: dictionary order;
Bazel re: `query` output sorting; basically all the POSIX utility specs;
C/C++ re: struct layout; all the non-normative notes in ECMAScript and C
and C++).

The concrete reason that I mentioned this is that some known data
provider implementations include “newest and `k - 1` uniform” while
others include “newest, oldest, and `k - 2` uniform”. Both of these are
fine. If we omit this sentence (“…may choose…”), then the latter kind of
implementation becomes invalid, because the points other than the newest
are clearly not selected uniformly at random.

If the implication is that the contract must specify every observable
behavior of the interface, then I must disagree. Every such constraint
by definition precludes some implementations, and often includes entire
classes of implementations with desirable properties. See above list for
lots of precedent.

The general approach to testing we need is that provided the given inputs/user settings that we properly configure the parent "ddtrace" logger as expected.

We should test that the logger gets configured via `ddtrace-run` or when manually importing `ddtrace`.

When testing with `ddtrace-run`, do not import `ddtrace` so we can validate that the logger gets setup ok on it's own from `ddtrace-run`.

We do not need to assert that other components in the ddtrace package (span, writer, tracer, etc) are logging properly those tests should exist in those component's tests.

We do not need to assert that Python standard library classes/functions are working as expected (e.g. does `RotatingFileHandler` actually rotate the logs?), but we do want to be sure that our logger is setup properly and works as expected (does it actually write to a file?)

Some quick timing comparisons:

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

N = 8760
data = np.arange(1, N)
idx = pd.date_range('2019-01-01', freq='h', periods=len(data))
rainfall = pd.Series(data, idx)

def f1():
    return (rainfall.index - rainfall.index.shift(-1)).total_seconds()

def f2():
    prior_ts = (rainfall.index[0] - (rainfall.index[1] - rainfall.index[0]))
    # prepend added in np 1.16.0
    dt_sec = np.diff(rainfall.index.astype(int), prepend=prior_ts.timestamp()*1e9) * 1e-9
    return dt_sec

def f3():
    first_diff = (rainfall.index[1] - rainfall.index[0]).total_seconds()
    dt_sec = np.ediff1d(rainfall.index.astype(int) * 1e-9, to_begin=first_diff)
    return dt_sec

def f4():
    first_diff = (rainfall.index[1] - rainfall.index[0]).total_seconds()*1e9
    dt_sec = np.ediff1d(rainfall.index.astype(int), to_begin=first_diff)*1e-9
    return dt_sec
```

```
%timeit f1()
403 µs ± 3.65 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit f2()
161 µs ± 6.25 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

%timeit f3()
401 µs ± 1.11 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

%timeit f4()
139 µs ± 1.34 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

`np.diff` is faster, at least on my machine, but the problem with is that it drops the unknown value instead of keeping it as `nan` so you have to kludge in a temporary value with the `prepend` argument.  However, `prepend` was only added in numpy 1.16.0, and our lowest supported version is currently 1.12.  There is an alternative method `np.ediff1d` that also shows the speed increase.  But anyway they're all well under a millisecond for an 8760, so IMO the speed isn't really relevant and code clarity is more important. 

Interestingly the timing changes dramatically whether you rescale by 1e9 before or after the diff (f3 vs f4)... strange.  I don't think it's the difference between diffing arrays with dtype=int or dtype=float.  Maybe it's somehow the difference between fitting in CPU cache or not.

That was my first implementation. The problem is how Python handles cases where a [comparison method](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__lt__) returns `NotImplemented`. In such a case, it seems to try the reflected method (e.g. `__lt__` vs. `__gt__`) on the other object. E.g. if we have

    `a_seconds_object < 1.0`

`a_seconds_object.__lt__(1.0)` returns `NotImplemented, so Python tries `1.0.__gt__(a_seconds_object)`, which would work if `Seconds` is a subclass of `float`.

Implementing `Seconds` by encapsulating instead of inheriting from `float` lets us define the behaviour exactly as we want it, with the drawback that we need to implement all the methods ([`functools.total_ordering`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.total_ordering) could help with that, though).

Thanks for the refinements, everyone. My motivation for changing this is primarily a Google-internal one. Our C++ code is generally compiled with exceptions disabled - throwing an exception results in `SIGABRT`. For most uses of exceptions in `pybind11`, this is not an issue because exceptions are localized to Python interface code, and so we can compile that single module with exceptions enabled. But this particular `bad_weak_ptr` exception would be thrown by code in `libc` and so we can't rely on that having been built with exceptions enabled. Hence the need for avoiding the exception in 'normal' code paths.

More general I think the problem you are trying to solve can't be solved the way you are trying. That is how I got suspicious and tried to understand how it is working. Ignoring for a second the separator `,` you are effectively trying to match something like this (.*)(a*)(.*) where I replaced `(xxx=yyy)*` with `.*` and `(sentry=yyy)*` with `a*`. The problem is that `a*` is a special case of `.*` and since * is greedy (.*)(a*)(.*) it is always going to match everything in the first group and will leave the last two groups empty. In your case that doesn't happen because of the way you match `,` but that causes other problems.

The way I would approach it is to look for `sentry=yyy` and then concatenate what is before and after taking care of removing one `,` if needed. That would perhaps preserve some invalid third party states but there is no real harm in that (it is not our concern and as long as we don't damage anything else there is no harm done).

If you want to be more rigorous you can match all `xxx=yyyy` and manually look among all matches for 'sentry=yyy' and put it in front.

Re: the scope of the data structure, I did briefly think about which fields to include and it seemed like a more semantically coherent boundary to define it as the data that will be committed.  Defining as just "everything except files" felt odd to me even if functionally it's the most convenient.  In particular even though cloning `run` is a bit ugly, semantically it just feels quite weird to make `run` a field on `self.data` instead of the top-level `RunLoader`, so I guess I preferred putting the weirdness inside the method bodies versus into the struct layout.  Similarly, it seems like semantically `self.checksum` would be more naturally located alongside `self.files` since it only affects the file-reading half of `RunLoader`, not the data-buffering half.

I guess in the purest sense we want something like a tripartite structure in which we have "effectively constant metadata" like `self.run` and `self.checksum` grouped in one substructure, and and then two different mutable substructures, `self.files` and `self.data`, so that all three can be reborrowed independently from the parent struct, but that seems... overcomplicated?  Would be nice to have some way to help the borrow checker resolve this without as much reshuffling of the actual struct fields themselves.

The main thread will be blocked on `future.get()`, but my understanding is that the `promise.set_value()` is executed by grpc on its private executors since it is a reply callback. The alternative to future is to use the sync API, but it is actually the same under the hood: sync API calls async API, and then waits for the future anyway.

I might suggest we change the signature of this utility function such that it's more generic and reusable elsewhere if needed.  Other Python objects besides dicts can be serialized into valid JSON-encoded strings.

The simplest way to accomplish this would probably be to allow this function accept any amount of objects (in arg and/or kwarg form) and check them all in one go by creating a simple tuple out of them to pass to `json.dumps`.

```python
def validate_json_serializable(*args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
    try:
         json.dumps((args, kwargs))
     except ...
```

This also removes the burden on the function caller to convert what are potentially multiple objects into a single data structure beforehand, which, for example, would also allow us to trim a line above where it is being used where we are doing exactly that:

```python
function_args = {"args": args, "kwargs": kwargs}
validate_json_serializable(function_args)
```

becomes:

```python
validate_json_serializable(*args, **kwargs)
```

Alternatively, we could just broaden the function definition and leave all else as-is:

```python
def validate_json_serializable(obj: Any) -> None:
    try:
        json.dumps(obj)
    except ...
```

I slightly prefer the former as it doesn't require the user to have to potentially convert multiple objects into a single data structure before using the function (like we are having to do now, by converting args+kwargs to a dict before passing to this function).  WDYT?

> There is no dependency between the pages. Just a dependency between the steps of each page. A failed page just breaks its follow-up steps.

Yes, the dependency can go away when the METS is not a shared resource anymore, but a server.

> Note that in case multiple pages (pages range) is passed to a single step (within a Nextflow process block), then a single broken page will also break the process block of that step and the follow up steps of all pages in the range.

Currently `is_type_comment()` takes a `suffix` argument, which is always passed as either `""` or `" ignore"`. Calls that use the former should stay as `is_type_coment()`, and calls that use the latter use `is_type_ignore_comment()`.

What's deprecated-ish is using type comments for type annotations like `x: []  # type: List[str]`. This was a compatibility feature introduced in PEP 484 for compatibility with Python 2 and Python 3.5, but it's not needed in modern versions of Python. Therefore, we're thinking of deprecating mypy support for it: python/mypy#12947. However, `# type: ignore` has no such replacement and it's here to stay.

Here's some benchmarks for both functions tested on Python 3.10.5, let me know what you think

```
$ ./percent_encode_urllib_parse_benchmark 
short(16ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 4.4 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 2.53 usec per loop

long(256ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 12.1 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 18 usec per loop

large(4096ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 77.9 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 255 usec per loop
```

```
$ ./percent_encode_urllib_parse_benchmark --plus
short(16ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 5.39 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 2.59 usec per loop

long(256ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 15 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 17.9 usec per loop

large(4096ch):
pydantic.utils.percent_encode: 50000 loops, best of 5: 79.5 usec per loop
urllib.parse.quote:            50000 loops, best of 5: 260 usec per loop
```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import random
import string
import sys
import timeit

TIMEIT_ARGV = ["-n", "50000", "-u", "usec"]

if "--plus" not in sys.argv:
    quote_timeit_argv = TIMEIT_ARGV + ["--setup", "from urllib.parse import quote"]
    percent_encode_timeit_argv = TIMEIT_ARGV + ["--setup", "from pydantic.utils import percent_encode"]
else:
    quote_timeit_argv = TIMEIT_ARGV + ["--setup", "from urllib.parse import quote_plus as quote"]
    percent_encode_timeit_argv = TIMEIT_ARGV + [
        "--setup",
        ";".join(
            [
                "from functools import partial",
                "from pydantic.utils import percent_encode",
                "percent_encode = partial(percent_encode, plus=True)",
            ]
        ),
    ]


def benchmark(string: str) -> None:
    print("pydantic.utils.percent_encode: ", end="")
    timeit.main(percent_encode_timeit_argv + [f"percent_encode({string!r}, plus=True)"])
    print("urllib.parse.quote:            ", end="")
    timeit.main(quote_timeit_argv + [f"quote({string!r})"])


SHORT_STRING = "".join(random.choices(string.printable, k=16))
LONG_STRING = "".join(random.choices(string.printable, k=256))
LARGE_STRING = "".join(random.choices(string.printable, k=4096))

print(f"short({len(SHORT_STRING)}ch):")
benchmark(SHORT_STRING)

print(f"\nlong({len(LONG_STRING)}ch):")
benchmark(LONG_STRING)

print(f"\nlarge({len(LARGE_STRING)}ch):")
benchmark(LARGE_STRING)
```

"Is this a real thing - radiance with slit function?"

Yes, it's the typical output for OES spectra. In RADIS it's created after using [s.apply_slit()](https://radis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/source/radis.spectrum.spectrum.html#radis.spectrum.spectrum.Spectrum.apply_slit)   or directly [convolve_with_slit(w, I, w_slit, I_slit, ...)](https://radis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/source/radis.tools.slit.html#radis.tools.slit.convolve_with_slit)


Applying a slit is not so obvious : the slit unit may be different from the spectrum default unit (in OES slits are measured in "nm", but Radis LBL computes in cm-1)  ; in dispersive spectroscopy the instrumental function may even change with the wavelength, because the grating reciprocal dispersion function changes  (in which case you have to apply the slit function "by-part").

That's why apply_slit exists and does all the work. 

In the future we want this to happen in the GPU to be able to do fitting ; but in a first approach I think it would be safer/more robust to have the GPU compute the spectrum only ; and let the CPU apply the slit with apply_slit.

Something like : 
```python
from radis.spectrum.utils import CONVOLUTED_QUANTITIES
if var in CONVOLUTED_QUANTITIES:
    s.update(var+"_noslit")    # gets the spectral array, more on this later
    s = s.take(var+"_noslit").apply_slit(...)   # only apply the slit to the final array 
else:
    s.update(var)      # gets the required spectral array 
```

The COG options for overviews are `AUTO/IGNORE_EXISTING/FORCE_USE_EXISTING/NONE`. We don't want to create our own overviews so we don't need FORCE_USE_EXISTING; we won't be creating our own overviews so we don't need IGNORE_EXISTING; AUTO is implied; so that only leaves NONE. We can assume most people don't want NONE so by converting `overviews=[]` to `overviews=None` internally we prevent creation of overviews in trollimage and we get auto overviews from COG. If someone does want a COG without overviews they probably don't need the COG driver (you can get a GeoTIFF to validate as a COG much easier if you don't have overviews).

By omitting the ICs for internal-only PEXes, they are created with the desired interpreter but otherwise unconstrained at runtime. I.E: There is no trace left in the PEX file output (PEX-INFO in particular) of the ICs. As such an internal_only PEX file built with IC "==3.7.** will produce the same PEX file as one built with IC "==3.8.*" if the PEX requirements happen to be agnostic to that difference.

Concretely, if we build a setuptools PEX with IC "==3.7.*" 1st, then we build one with IC "==3.8.*" we get the same output PEX which leads downstream rules, notably VenvPex reliant ones, missing an encoding of the IC. For a normal internal_only PEX file, we have the same problem, but we run them via `python pex_file ...` where the `python` is the one picked from the otherwise erased ICs. In the VenvPex case, we can do no such thing since a venv always (must) uses its interpreter.
